extra.message: User-controlled 'name' parameter from request.args is rendered with the '| safe' filter, bypassing Jinja2's auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via ?name=<script>alert(1)</script>.

User-controlled 'name' from cookies or POST form data is rendered with the '| safe' filter, bypassing Jinja2's auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript by setting a cookie or submitting a form with a malicious name value.

Stored database values (message.from_user, message.message, message.date) are rendered with the '| safe' filter in the admin messages view. User-controlled data submitted via the feedback form (account_area/views.py:75) or API (api/views.py:52) is stored in the Message table and then rendered unsafely to administrators, enabling stored XSS.

The currently logged-in user's username (g.user.username) is rendered with the '| safe' filter, bypassing Jinja2 auto-escaping. If a user with a malicious username (e.g., created via the admin panel) logs in, arbitrary JavaScript executes in the base template shared across all pages.

X-XSS-Protection header is explicitly set to 0 (disabled) for all responses when the session flag 'xss_protection_enabled' is False (the default). This disables the browser's built-in reflected XSS filter, making XSS exploitation easier.

The 'next' query parameter from user input is passed directly to Flask's redirect() without validation. An attacker can craft a URL like /toggle_xss_protection/?next=https://evil.com to redirect users to an arbitrary external site (open redirect / phishing).

Flask's SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'Keep it secret, keep it safe' in the source code. This allows anyone with access to the code to forge session cookies, potentially leading to session hijacking or privilege escalation.

Flask DEBUG mode is enabled. This exposes the Werkzeug debugger and full stack traces to users on error, potentially leaking sensitive source code, configuration, and environment details. In production, this should be disabled.

Passwords are stored and compared in plaintext (user.password == password). No hashing (bcrypt, argon2, etc.) is used. An attacker with database access can read all user passwords. Additionally, the User.serialize property (models.py:27) exposes the password field in API responses.

cwe: CWE-256: Plaintext Storage of a Password

extra.message: The API POST endpoint (api/views.py:43-56) accepts 'message' and 'from_user' from request.get_json() without any sanitization and stores them in the Message table. These values are later rendered unsafely with | safe in admin/message.html:29-30, enabling stored XSS against administrators who view messages.

User feedback from request.form (line 66) is stored in the Message model without sanitization. This user-controlled 'feedback' value is later rendered with the | safe filter in admin/message.html:30, enabling stored XSS against admin users viewing messages.

The 'name' cookie is set without httponly=True, secure=True, or samesite flags. This makes the cookie accessible to JavaScript (enabling cookie theft via XSS) and allows transmission over unencrypted HTTP connections.

The change_password, change_email, and send_feedback endpoints accept POST requests without any CSRF token validation. An attacker can craft a cross-site request that changes the victim's password, email, or sends feedback on their behalf.
